content.problemStatement:
Chef has been invited to be a judge for a cycling race.

You may think about cyclists in the race as points on a line. All the cyclists start at the same point with an initial speed of zero. All of them move in the same direction. Some cyclists may force their cycles to speed up. The change of speed takes place immediately. The rest of the time, they move with a constant speed. There are **N** cyclists in total, numbered from **1** to **N**.

In order to award points to the participants, Chef wants to know how far the cyclists have reached at certain points of time.

Corresponding to the above two, there will be two types of queries.


Change the speed of cyclist **i** at some time.
Ask for the position of the race leader at some time.
There are **Q** queries. Each query of first type is described by the type of the query (1), the time **Time**, the cyclist number **cyclist**, and the new speed **NewSpeed**. Each query of second type is described by the type of the query (2), and the time **Time**.

Initial speed of all the cyclists is 0.
constraints: The speed of any cyclist never decreases.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and Q — the number of cyclists and the number of queries. Then Q lines follow. Each line starts with an integer Type.
- If Type = 1, three more integers follow: Time, cyclist, NewSpeed — meaning that at moment Time the speed of cyclist (1‑based index) becomes NewSpeed. The speed never decreases.
- If Type = 2, one more integer follows: Time — you have to output the position of the race leader (the maximum distance travelled by any cyclist) at moment Time.
All times are non‑negative and fit in 32‑bit signed integer range.
content.outputFormat: For each query of type 2, output a single line containing the maximum distance (as a 64‑bit integer) reached by any cyclist at the given time, in the order the queries appear in the input.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The position of the race leader at each time is calculated based on the speed of the cyclists.

solution.approach:
All speed changes and queries are processed in non‑decreasing order of time. For each cyclist we keep:
- lastSpeed – the speed after the most recent change,
- lastTime  – the moment of that change,
- distSoFar – total distance travelled up to lastTime.
When a speed change at time t is met, the segment that was valid from lastTime to t is a line f(x)=a+ b·x with slope b = lastSpeed and intercept a = distSoFar - b·lastTime. This line is added to a Li Chao segment tree on the interval [lastTime, t]. Afterwards we update distSoFar += lastSpeed·(t‑lastTime), set lastSpeed = NewSpeed and lastTime = t, and insert the new line (valid from t to +∞) into the tree on interval [t, MAX_TIME].
For a query at time t we simply ask the Li Chao tree for the maximum value among all stored lines at x = t. The answer is the leader's distance.
The Li Chao tree works on the whole x‑range [0, 10^9] and supports adding a line on a sub‑interval in O(log X) time, where X is the coordinate range. Both updates and queries are O(log X), so the total complexity is O((Q+N)·log X). All arithmetic uses 64‑bit signed integers.
Edge cases handled:
- Multiple events having the same time – they are processed in the order they appear, which is correct because a speed change at time t influences the distance from that exact moment onward.
- Queries before any speed change – the tree initially contains a line with slope 0 and intercept 0 for every cyclist, giving distance 0.
- Very large times – the tree range is fixed to the maximum possible time (10^9).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + number_of_lines) ≤ O(Q) for the Li Chao tree nodes
